subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: At trial for assault, the Crown seeks to call a witness to testify that the victim told her, one hour after the alleged assault, 'He hit me in the face.' The victim is deceased and unavailable to testify. Defence objects that the statement is hearsay.
call_of_question: Under the principled approach to hearsay (R. v. Khan), on what basis may the trial judge admit this out-of-court statement?

options.A: The statement is automatically admissible because the victim is unavailable to testify.
options.B: The statement may be admitted under the principled approach if the Crown demonstrates that it meets the twin criteria of necessity and threshold reliability.
options.C: The statement is admissible only if it falls within a recognized traditional hearsay exception such as dying declarations.
options.D: The statement must be excluded because the accused has the right under s. 7 of the Charter to confront all witnesses who give evidence against them.

explanation: Under the principled approach to hearsay developed in R. v. Khan, [1990] 2 SCR 531 and refined in R. v. Starr, 2000 SCC 40, out-of-court statements that do not fall within a traditional exception may nonetheless be admitted if the party seeking admission establishes: (1) necessity — the declarant is unavailable or it would be reasonably impractical to call them, and (2) threshold reliability — sufficient guarantees of reliability to justify admission without cross-examination (either circumstantial reliability or procedural reliability). The unavailability of the victim satisfies necessity. The court must then assess threshold reliability based on the circumstances of the statement.
why_A_wrong: Unavailability satisfies necessity, but necessity alone does not make hearsay admissible. The principled approach also requires threshold reliability. Automatic admission on the basis of unavailability would admit unreliable hearsay.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under the Khan/Starr principled approach, hearsay may be admitted if the Crown demonstrates necessity and threshold reliability.
why_C_wrong: The principled approach is not limited to traditional exceptions. Under Khan, courts may admit hearsay outside traditional exceptions if necessity and threshold reliability are established. The rigid categorical approach was replaced by the principled framework.
why_D_wrong: Section 7 of the Charter does not create an absolute right to confront every declarant. The principled hearsay approach and the accused's right to cross-examination are balanced: if threshold reliability is high and necessity is established, admission does not automatically violate s.7.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Hearsay — Principled Approach
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Khan, [1990] 2 SCR 531; R. v. Starr, 2000 SCC 40; R. v. Khelawon, 2006 SCC 57

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The Crown seeks to admit a statement that the complainant made to a police officer two days after the alleged sexual assault. The statement was recorded on video. The complainant is alive and available to testify but refuses to cooperate. The trial judge must assess threshold reliability.
call_of_question: Under R. v. Khelawon, 2006 SCC 57, which of the following BEST describes how the trial judge should assess threshold reliability?

options.A: By assessing whether the statement, if true, would be sufficient to prove the offence beyond a reasonable doubt.
options.B: By weighing the ultimate reliability of the statement on the balance of probabilities.
options.C: By examining the circumstances surrounding the making of the statement to determine whether there are adequate substitutes for the oath, presence, and cross-examination that the trier of fact would normally have.
options.D: By considering only whether the statement was made under oath or solemn affirmation.

explanation: In R. v. Khelawon, 2006 SCC 57, the Supreme Court clarified that threshold reliability is assessed by examining the circumstances surrounding the making of the statement — what the court calls 'circumstantial guarantees of reliability.' The question is whether those circumstances provide adequate substitutes for the three traditional safeguards of in-court testimony: the oath, the presence of the trier of fact (who observes demeanour), and cross-examination. Alternatively, the statement may be admitted based on procedural reliability if the accused has the opportunity to test the evidence. The judge does not assess ultimate reliability (that is for the trier of fact) or require proof beyond a reasonable doubt.
why_A_wrong: Threshold reliability is not assessed by asking whether the statement proves the offence beyond a reasonable doubt. That conflates the admissibility threshold with the ultimate question for the trier of fact. The judge assesses the circumstances, not the ultimate sufficiency of the evidence.
why_B_wrong: The trial judge does not weigh ultimate reliability on a balance of probabilities at the threshold stage. Ultimate reliability is for the trier of fact. Threshold reliability concerns the circumstances providing adequate safeguards for the statement's truth.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Threshold reliability under Khelawon requires examining the circumstances surrounding the making of the statement for adequate substitutes for the three traditional in-court safeguards.
why_D_wrong: Being under oath is one circumstantial factor but not the exclusive test for threshold reliability. Many reliable out-of-court statements are not made under oath. The analysis is holistic and considers all surrounding circumstances.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Hearsay — Threshold Reliability (Khelawon)
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Khelawon, 2006 SCC 57; R. v. Starr, 2000 SCC 40

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Prior Sexual Activity Evidence — s. 276 Criminal Code
fact_pattern: The accused is charged with sexual assault. He wishes to introduce evidence at trial that the complainant had consensual sexual intercourse with him on a prior occasion. He argues this prior sexual activity is relevant to whether the complainant consented on the occasion charged.
call_of_question: Under s. 276 of the Criminal Code, which of the following is the correct procedure for admitting this evidence?

options.A: The evidence is automatically admissible because prior consent is directly relevant to the issue of consent on the occasion charged.
options.B: The evidence is automatically inadmissible under s. 276; prior sexual activity can never be adduced in a sexual assault trial.
options.C: The accused must bring a s. 276 application before the trial judge; the judge holds a voir dire and may admit the evidence only if it meets the specific criteria in s. 276(2).
options.D: The accused may adduce the prior sexual activity evidence as long as he gives notice to the Crown at least 7 days before trial.

explanation: Section 276 of the Criminal Code creates a two-step process for prior sexual activity evidence. Section 276(1) prohibits admission of such evidence to support twin myths: (a) that the complainant is more likely to have consented, or (b) that she is less credible. Section 276(2) provides that prior sexual activity evidence is admissible only if: (a) it relates to a specific instance of sexual activity; (b) it is relevant to an issue at trial; (c) it has significant probative value that is not substantially outweighed by the danger of prejudice; and (d) it does not engage the twin myths. The procedure under s. 276.1 requires the accused to bring a written application, serve it on the Crown and complainant's counsel, and the judge holds a voir dire before ruling on admissibility.
why_A_wrong: Prior sexual activity is not automatically admissible. Section 276(1) specifically prohibits using it to support the twin myths. Admission requires a s. 276 application and satisfaction of the s. 276(2) criteria.
why_B_wrong: Section 276 does not categorically exclude all prior sexual activity evidence. Section 276(2) permits admission in specific circumstances, subject to the prescribed criteria and procedure. The prohibition is against the twin myths, not all prior sexual activity.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 276.1 requires a written application and voir dire before the trial judge, who may admit the evidence only upon satisfaction of s. 276(2) criteria.
why_D_wrong: A 7-day notice to the Crown alone is insufficient. Section 276.1 requires a formal written application served on both the Crown and the complainant's counsel, followed by a voir dire in which the judge applies the s. 276(2) criteria before ruling on admissibility.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Prior Sexual Activity Evidence — s. 276
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 276, s. 276.1, s. 276.2; R. v. Seaboyer, [1991] 2 SCR 577

tested_concepts: judicial voir dire for prior sexual activity

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Prior Sexual Activity Evidence — s. 276 Criminal Code
fact_pattern: In a sexual assault trial, the accused brings a s. 276 application to admit evidence that physical injuries to the complainant's body were caused by her other consensual sexual activity with a third party occurring hours before the alleged assault. The accused argues the evidence directly rebuts the Crown's use of the injuries as proof of non-consensual force.
call_of_question: Which s. 276(2) criterion is MOST important to the admissibility of this specific prior sexual activity evidence?

options.A: That the prior sexual activity involved the same accused.
options.B: That the evidence has significant probative value that is not substantially outweighed by the danger of prejudice to the proper administration of justice.
options.C: That the complainant consented to disclosure of the prior sexual activity.
options.D: That the evidence is corroborated by independent witnesses.

explanation: Section 276(2)(c) requires that the evidence have 'significant probative value that is not substantially outweighed by the danger of prejudice to the proper administration of justice.' On these facts, where the prior sexual activity may directly explain physical evidence used by the Crown as proof of non-consensual force, the probative value of explaining the injuries is highly significant. This is an alternative explanation for physical evidence — not a twin myths argument — and may satisfy s. 276(2)(b) (relevance to an issue at trial) and s. 276(2)(c) (probative value). The balancing of significant probative value against danger of prejudice is the critical criterion in this context, as the defence is not seeking to attack credibility but to rebut specific physical evidence.
why_A_wrong: Section 276(2) does not require that the prior sexual activity involved the same accused. The accused may seek to introduce evidence of prior sexual activity with a third party, provided the s. 276(2) criteria are met.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 276(2)(c)'s balancing of significant probative value against danger of prejudice is the most important criterion when the prior sexual activity provides an alternative explanation for physical evidence used by the Crown.
why_C_wrong: Section 276(2) does not require the complainant's consent to disclosure. The provision protects the complainant's privacy and dignity interests through the twin myths prohibition and probative/prejudice balancing, but it does not require consent as an admissibility criterion.
why_D_wrong: Section 276(2) does not require independent corroboration of the prior sexual activity as a condition of admissibility. The accused's application may rely on the complainant's own accounts or other evidence, without independent corroboration being a prerequisite.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Prior Sexual Activity — s. 276(2) Criteria
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 276(2)(b), s. 276(2)(c); R. v. Darrach, 2000 SCC 46

tested_concepts: s. 276(2)(c) probative value vs prejudice balancing
non-myth use of prior sexual activity

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The Crown calls a key witness who, at trial, gives testimony that contradicts a statement he gave police six months earlier. The Crown wishes to use the earlier police statement to challenge the witness's credibility and potentially to prove the truth of its contents.
call_of_question: Under R. v. K.G.B., [1993] 1 SCR 740, under what circumstances may the prior inconsistent statement be used as substantive evidence (i.e., for the truth of its contents)?

options.A: Never — prior inconsistent statements can only be used to impeach credibility, never for the truth of their contents.
options.B: Only if the witness adopts the prior statement as true at trial.
options.C: If the prior statement was made under oath or solemn affirmation and was video-recorded or otherwise memorialized, and the witness has the opportunity to explain the inconsistency.
options.D: If the trial judge finds, on a balance of probabilities, that the prior statement is more reliable than the in-court testimony.

explanation: In R. v. K.G.B., [1993] 1 SCR 740, the Supreme Court of Canada held that a prior inconsistent statement may be admitted as substantive evidence — for the truth of its contents — under the principled approach to hearsay if two conditions are met: (1) the statement was made under oath or solemn affirmation (or the equivalent), and (2) the statement was video-recorded or there is a reliable record of it; AND the witness has a meaningful opportunity to explain or deny the inconsistency in cross-examination. These requirements are functionally equivalent to the oath, observation by the trier of fact, and the ability to cross-examine — the traditional guarantees of reliability that are absent from simple out-of-court statements. Where these criteria are met, the statement may go to the truth of its contents, not just to impeach credibility.
why_A_wrong: After K.G.B., this categorical rule was rejected. Prior inconsistent statements meeting the K.G.B. criteria may be admitted for their truth, not merely to impeach. The old rule that restricted prior inconsistent statements to impeachment was overruled.
why_B_wrong: If the witness adopts the prior statement as their current testimony at trial, the statement becomes part of the witness's in-court evidence. But K.G.B. provides a broader route: admission of prior statements for their truth without adoption, provided the oath/record criteria are met.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under K.G.B., prior inconsistent statements may be admitted as substantive evidence if they were made under oath/solemn affirmation and were video-recorded (or equivalently memorialized), and the witness has the opportunity to explain the inconsistency.
why_D_wrong: The trial judge does not weigh ultimate reliability on a balance of probabilities to admit the prior statement. The K.G.B. criteria are procedural (oath + record); if met, the statement goes to the trier of fact, who weighs its reliability. The judge does not pre-determine which version is more reliable.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Prior Inconsistent Statements — K.G.B.
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. K.G.B., [1993] 1 SCR 740; Canada Evidence Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-5, s. 9-11

tested_concepts: K.G.B. requirements for substantive use of prior inconsistent statements
distinction between impeachment and substantive use

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: At trial for robbery, the Crown calls the complainant. The complainant now testifies that he is 'not sure' who robbed him, contradicting a detailed statement he gave police four days after the robbery, which was neither made under oath nor video-recorded. The Crown applies under s. 9(2) of the Canada Evidence Act to cross-examine its own witness on the prior inconsistent statement.
call_of_question: If the trial judge permits cross-examination under s. 9(2), to what use may the prior inconsistent statement be put?

options.A: It may be used as substantive evidence of the truth of its contents, because s. 9(2) operates as a K.G.B. exception.
options.B: It may be used only to impeach the credibility of the Crown's own witness, not for the truth of its contents.
options.C: It may be used for both credibility and substantive purposes if the trial judge finds it more reliable than the in-court testimony.
options.D: It may not be used at all once the witness recants.

explanation: Section 9(2) of the Canada Evidence Act permits the Crown (or any party) to cross-examine its own witness on a prior inconsistent written statement or one proved to have been made, after obtaining leave of the court and establishing the inconsistency. However, a prior inconsistent statement used under s. 9(2) does not automatically become substantive evidence (admitted for the truth of its contents). It may be used only to impeach credibility — to demonstrate that the witness gave a prior inconsistent account and therefore to diminish the reliability of their current testimony. To be used substantively, the statement would need to meet the K.G.B. principled approach criteria (oath + record), which this statement does not, since it was not made under oath or video-recorded.
why_A_wrong: Section 9(2) does not make a prior inconsistent statement automatically admissible for the truth of its contents. K.G.B. provides a separate route for substantive admission, requiring oath and video-record (or equivalent). A s. 9(2) statement not meeting K.G.B. criteria may only be used to impeach.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. A prior inconsistent statement admitted under s. 9(2) that does not meet K.G.B. criteria may only be used to impeach the witness's credibility, not for the truth of its contents.
why_C_wrong: The trial judge's assessment of which version is 'more reliable' is not the test for substantive use. The K.G.B. criteria (oath, record) are threshold requirements. The judge's reliability preference does not convert a credibility tool into substantive evidence.
why_D_wrong: The prior inconsistent statement may still be used even when the witness recants or gives a contradictory account at trial — that is precisely the scenario for which s. 9(2) is available. Recantation triggers the right to apply to cross-examine the witness on the prior statement.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Prior Inconsistent Statements — s. 9(2) Canada Evidence Act
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canada Evidence Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-5, s. 9(2); R. v. K.G.B., [1993] 1 SCR 740; R. v. Milgaard, [1971] 2 CCC (2d) 206

tested_concepts: distinction between credibility tool and truth evidence

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The accused is charged with fraud. The Crown seeks to introduce evidence that the accused committed three similar frauds five years ago for which he was convicted. The Crown argues the evidence is relevant to show a 'system' or 'design' and to rebut the accused's claim that the current alleged fraud was an innocent mistake.
call_of_question: Under the similar fact evidence rule (R. v. Handy, 2002 SCC 56), the trial judge must determine whether to admit this evidence. Which of the following correctly states the admissibility test?

options.A: Similar fact evidence is automatically admissible when the accused has prior convictions for the same type of offence.
options.B: Similar fact evidence is admissible only if the accused takes the stand and puts their character in issue.
options.C: The trial judge must weigh the probative value of the evidence against its prejudicial effect; it is admissible only if the probative value substantially outweighs the prejudicial effect.
options.D: Similar fact evidence is always inadmissible in fraud cases because of the risk of moral reasoning (propensity reasoning).

explanation: In R. v. Handy, 2002 SCC 56, the Supreme Court of Canada set out the framework for similar fact evidence. The general rule is that evidence of prior bad acts is inadmissible if its only relevance is to show disposition or propensity ('this person did it before; therefore they did it again'). However, similar fact evidence is admissible if its probative value substantially outweighs its prejudicial effect. Probative value includes the strength of the inference the evidence supports beyond mere propensity, such as proving system, design, identity, or rebutting accident/mistake. Prejudicial effect includes the risk that the jury will reason from general bad character rather than the specific evidence. The judge conducts this balancing on a voir dire and admits the evidence only where probative value substantially outweighs prejudice.
why_A_wrong: Similar fact evidence is not automatically admissible because of prior convictions for the same type of offence. Prior convictions are subject to the same probative value vs. prejudicial effect balancing test. Automatic admission would impermissibly invite propensity reasoning.
why_B_wrong: The Crown may apply to admit similar fact evidence whether or not the accused takes the stand. The similar fact evidence rule operates as an exception to the propensity rule regardless of whether the accused puts their character in issue.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under Handy, similar fact evidence is admissible only if its probative value substantially outweighs its prejudicial effect, after a judicial balancing exercise.
why_D_wrong: Similar fact evidence is not categorically inadmissible in fraud cases. The Handy balancing test applies to all cases. Evidence that proves 'system' or 'design' in fraud may have substantial probative value and, if it substantially outweighs the prejudicial effect, is admissible.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Similar Fact Evidence
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Handy, 2002 SCC 56; R. v. Makin, [1894] AC 57

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Business Records Exception — s. 30 Canada Evidence Act
fact_pattern: In a fraud trial, the Crown seeks to admit records of a bank's daily transaction logs without calling each bank employee who created the entries. Crown argues these are admissible as business records under s. 30 of the Canada Evidence Act. Defence objects that the original authors of the entries must testify.
call_of_question: Under s. 30 of the Canada Evidence Act, which of the following conditions must be established for the transaction logs to be admitted as business records?

options.A: The Crown must call the employee who made each individual entry to authenticate the record.
options.B: The records must be produced from the originator's personal computer only and must not be electronically transmitted copies.
options.C: The record must have been made in the usual and ordinary course of business, and the record must have been made at or near the time of the act, transaction, occurrence, or event it purports to record.
options.D: The records are admissible only if the original document is produced; copies are not admissible under s. 30.

explanation: Section 30 of the Canada Evidence Act creates an exception to the hearsay rule for business records. Under s. 30(1), a record made in the usual and ordinary course of business is admissible as evidence of the facts stated therein, provided: (a) the record was made in the usual and ordinary course of business; (b) it was the usual and ordinary course of that business to make such records; and (c) the record was made at or near the time of the act, transaction, occurrence, or event it purports to record. The calling of each individual employee who made an entry is not required — that is the very purpose of the business records exception. A party may produce a certificate of authenticity under s. 30(7) in lieu of the author's testimony.
why_A_wrong: One of the main purposes of the s. 30 business records exception is to avoid the need to call every employee who made a record entry. The exception removes the requirement to produce each author as a witness, provided the conditions of the section are met.
why_B_wrong: Section 30 does not restrict admissibility to original paper records or exclude electronic copies. The Canada Evidence Act has been updated to accommodate electronic records, and s. 30 applies to business records generally, including those stored and transmitted electronically.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 30 of the Canada Evidence Act requires that the record be made in the usual and ordinary course of business and at or near the time of the transaction or event it records.
why_D_wrong: Section 30 does not require production of the original document; it does not exclude copies. The Canada Evidence Act contains provisions addressing the admissibility of copies and electronic records.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Business Records Exception — s. 30 Canada Evidence Act
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canada Evidence Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-5, s. 30(1)

exam_trigger_words: usual and ordinary course of business
tested_concepts: s. 30 Canada Evidence Act business records exception
usual and ordinary course of business

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Prior Consistent Statements — Rule Against and Exceptions
fact_pattern: The Crown's key witness testifies about the robbery she witnessed. In cross-examination, defence counsel suggests the witness fabricated her account only after speaking to investigators. Crown counsel then seeks to introduce a statement the witness made to a friend the morning after the robbery — before she ever spoke to police — in which she described the accused as the perpetrator.
call_of_question: What is the general rule regarding prior consistent statements, and does an exception apply in this scenario?

options.A: Prior consistent statements are admissible as of right because they corroborate the witness's in-court testimony.
options.B: Prior consistent statements are generally inadmissible (self-serving hearsay), but an exception applies where the defence has alleged recent fabrication; the prior consistent statement may be admitted to rebut the fabrication allegation, provided it pre-dates the alleged motive to fabricate.
options.C: Prior consistent statements are admissible because they were made to a private individual rather than to police.
options.D: Prior consistent statements are inadmissible in all circumstances; the witness's credibility must be assessed only on in-court testimony.

explanation: The general rule in Canadian evidence law is that a party cannot introduce their own witness's prior statements consistent with the witness's in-court testimony — these are self-serving and inadmissible hearsay. However, one established exception applies where the opposing party has alleged recent fabrication — that is, alleged that the witness invented the story at some point after the alleged event, usually in response to a motive that arose after the event. Where such an allegation is made, the party calling the witness may introduce a prior consistent statement made before the alleged motive to fabricate arose. The purpose of the exception is to rebut the fabrication allegation, not to confirm the truth of the statement. On these facts, defence alleged the witness fabricated her account after speaking to investigators; the prior statement to her friend pre-dates that contact and falls within the exception.
why_A_wrong: Prior consistent statements are not admissible as of right for corroborative purposes. Self-corroboration is generally inadmissible. The general rule is that a witness cannot bolster their own credibility by reference to prior consistent statements without a triggering exception.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The recent fabrication exception allows admission of a prior consistent statement to rebut a fabrication allegation, provided the statement pre-dates the alleged motive to fabricate.
why_C_wrong: The identity of the recipient (friend vs. police) does not determine admissibility of prior consistent statements. The admissibility turns on the general rule against self-serving hearsay and the applicable exceptions, not on who received the statement.
why_D_wrong: Prior consistent statements are not inadmissible in all circumstances. The recent fabrication exception is well-established at common law and permits admission in specific circumstances. Other exceptions also exist (e.g., prior identification, spontaneous utterances).

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Prior Consistent Statements — Recent Fabrication Exception
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Stirling, 2008 SCC 10; R. v. O'Brien, [1978] 1 SCR 591 (recent fabrication exception)

tested_concepts: general rule against prior consistent statements
statement must pre-date alleged motive to fabricate
purpose — rebut fabrication not confirm truth

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The accused is charged with fraud. He testifies that he has been a respected businessman for 20 years and has never been involved in any dishonest dealings. He also calls three character witnesses who testify to his honest and trustworthy reputation in the community. The Crown then seeks to cross-examine the accused on a prior conviction for tax evasion.
call_of_question: When an accused puts their good character in evidence, what consequence does this have regarding their prior record?

options.A: Putting good character in evidence has no effect on the accused's prior record; the Crown can never cross-examine on prior convictions.
options.B: By putting their character in issue, the accused opens the door to cross-examination on prior convictions that are relevant to the good character claim, including convictions for dishonesty.
options.C: The accused who testifies may only be cross-examined on prior convictions if they are for offences of the same type as the charge.
options.D: Character witnesses may testify to reputation, but the accused personally cannot refer to their own character without the Crown's consent.

explanation: In Canadian criminal law, an accused is permitted to lead evidence of good character — both through personal testimony and through reputation witnesses. However, by placing their own character in issue (particularly their reputation for honesty), the accused opens the door to cross-examination on prior convictions that are relevant to that character claim. Under s. 12 of the Canada Evidence Act, an accused who testifies may be cross-examined on prior convictions. Where the accused has made claims of honest character, a prior conviction for tax evasion (which involves dishonesty) is directly relevant to rebut the good character claim and may be put to the accused in cross-examination. The prior conviction goes to credibility and to rebut the good character evidence.
why_A_wrong: Putting good character in evidence does not immunize the accused from cross-examination on prior convictions. Section 12 of the Canada Evidence Act permits cross-examination of an accused who testifies on prior convictions, and placing good character in issue makes such cross-examination particularly relevant.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. By putting their honesty and trustworthy character in issue, the accused opens the door to cross-examination on prior convictions relevant to that claim, including a prior tax evasion conviction.
why_C_wrong: Cross-examination on prior convictions is not limited to convictions for the same type of offence. Convictions relevant to the character claim put forward are fair game. An accused who claims honest character may be cross-examined on prior dishonesty convictions even if they are not the same offence type.
why_D_wrong: The accused may personally testify about their own good character without the Crown's consent. The self-character evidence rule permits an accused to adduce their own good character through personal testimony and through reputation witnesses. Crown consent is not required.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Character Evidence — Accused's Good Character
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Canada Evidence Act, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-5, s. 12; R. v. McNamara (No. 1) (1981), 56 CCC (2d) 193 (Ont. CA)

tested_concepts: opening the door to prior convictions
prior dishonesty conviction relevant to honesty claim

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The defence seeks to call a linguist as an expert witness to testify that the threatening letters sent to the victim share stylistic features inconsistent with those of the accused's known writing samples. The trial judge must determine whether to admit the expert evidence.
call_of_question: Under R. v. Mohan, [1994] 2 SCR 9, which of the following correctly sets out the four criteria for admissibility of expert evidence?

options.A: Relevance; necessity in assisting the trier of fact; absence of an exclusionary rule; and the proper qualification of the expert.
options.B: Relevance; reliability of the expert's methodology; independence of the expert; and sufficient peer review of the field.
options.C: The expert must hold a university degree in a relevant field; the opinion must be published in a peer-reviewed journal; the field must be generally accepted in the scientific community; and the evidence must be helpful to the trier of fact.
options.D: The field of expertise must be recognized by Canadian courts; the expert must be qualified by the judge; the expert must not offer opinion on the ultimate issue; and the probative value must outweigh prejudice.

explanation: In R. v. Mohan, [1994] 2 SCR 9, the Supreme Court of Canada set out four criteria for the admissibility of expert opinion evidence: (1) relevance — the evidence must be relevant to an issue in the case; (2) necessity in assisting the trier of fact — the evidence must be beyond the ordinary knowledge of the jury and necessary to assist them in reaching a decision; (3) the absence of any exclusionary rule — no rule of evidence must preclude the evidence; and (4) a properly qualified expert — the witness must be shown to possess special knowledge, experience, skill, or training relevant to the matter. These four criteria from Mohan remain the foundational framework, though subsequent cases like R. v. J.-L.J. and White Burgess Langille Inman have elaborated on the reliability/threshold reliability analysis within these criteria.
why_A_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The four Mohan criteria are: relevance, necessity in assisting the trier of fact, absence of an exclusionary rule, and a properly qualified expert.
why_B_wrong: While reliability of methodology is an important consideration developed in post-Mohan jurisprudence (particularly R. v. J.-L.J. and White Burgess), it is not one of the four original Mohan criteria. The original Mohan framework uses the four criteria listed in option A.
why_C_wrong: A university degree and peer-reviewed publication are not standalone Mohan criteria. The Mohan framework uses the four criteria in option A. The Daubert criteria from American law (general acceptance in the scientific community, peer review) influenced Canadian law but are not the Mohan test.
why_D_wrong: The 'no opinion on ultimate issue' rule was historically a common-law principle, but it is not one of the four Mohan criteria. Expert witnesses in Canada may in appropriate circumstances testify on the ultimate issue. The four Mohan criteria do not include a prohibition on ultimate issue opinions.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Expert Opinion Evidence — Mohan
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Mohan, [1994] 2 SCR 9

tested_concepts: Mohan four criteria for expert admissibility

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Admitting Prior Testimony at Trial — s. 715 Criminal Code
fact_pattern: At a preliminary inquiry, the complainant testified under oath and was cross-examined by defence counsel. By the time of trial, the complainant has died. Crown counsel applies to admit the transcript of the complainant's preliminary inquiry testimony at trial.
call_of_question: Under s. 715 of the Criminal Code, what conditions must be met for the transcript of the complainant's preliminary inquiry testimony to be admitted at trial?

options.A: The transcript may be admitted only if the defence consents to its admission at trial.
options.B: The transcript is admissible as of right because it was given under oath; no further conditions apply.
options.C: The transcript may be admitted if the witness is unavailable to testify at trial and: (a) the witness gave evidence at a prior proceeding under oath; (b) the accused was present at the prior proceeding; and (c) the accused had the opportunity to cross-examine the witness at the prior proceeding.
options.D: The transcript is admissible only if the prior testimony was given in the same trial, not in a preliminary inquiry.

explanation: Section 715(1) of the Criminal Code provides that where a witness who gave evidence at a preliminary inquiry or other prior proceeding is unavailable to testify at trial (due to death, illness, absence from Canada, or other sufficient cause), the evidence given at the prior proceeding may be admitted at trial if: (a) the evidence was taken down in a form that the accused would be required to answer at trial; (b) the accused or counsel was present at the prior proceeding; and (c) the accused or counsel had a full opportunity to cross-examine the witness. All three conditions are met on these facts: the evidence was given under oath at the preliminary inquiry, the accused was present with counsel, and defence had the opportunity to cross-examine. The complainant's death makes her unavailable, satisfying the unavailability requirement.
why_A_wrong: Defence consent is not a condition for admission under s. 715. The Crown may apply for admission and the court will grant it if the statutory conditions are met, regardless of whether the defence objects.
why_B_wrong: Being given under oath at a prior proceeding alone is not sufficient for automatic admission under s. 715. The presence of the accused and the opportunity to cross-examine are additional mandatory conditions. Without both, the testimony may not satisfy the s. 715 requirements.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 715(1) requires: witness unavailability at trial; evidence given under oath at prior proceeding; accused (or counsel) was present; and accused (or counsel) had the opportunity to cross-examine.
why_D_wrong: Section 715 expressly covers evidence from preliminary inquiries, prior trials, and other prior proceedings. It is not limited to prior testimony given in the same trial. The preliminary inquiry is one of the most common prior proceedings from which testimony is admitted under s. 715.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Prior Testimony at Trial — s. 715 Criminal Code
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 715(1)

tested_concepts: s. 715(1) conditions for prior testimony admission
presence of accused at prior proceeding

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Prior Identification — Prior Consistent Statement Exception
fact_pattern: At trial for robbery, the eyewitness testifies that she identified the accused in a police photo lineup conducted two days after the robbery. She picks out the accused's photo and says it is the person who robbed her. Crown seeks to have the officer who conducted the lineup testify that the witness identified the same photo.
call_of_question: Is evidence of the witness's prior photo lineup identification admissible, and if so, on what basis?

options.A: No — prior identification evidence is a prior consistent statement and is inadmissible on that basis.
options.B: Yes — prior identification is a recognized exception to the rule against prior consistent statements; the identifying officer may testify about the prior identification made by the witness.
options.C: Yes — but only if the identification occurred within 24 hours of the crime; later identifications are excluded as prior consistent statements.
options.D: No — the only admissible identification evidence is the witness's in-court identification of the accused.

explanation: Prior identification evidence — a witness's previous act of identifying the accused — is a well-recognized exception to the general rule against prior consistent statements. Prior identification is admissible both through the identifying witness and through officers or others who witnessed the identification procedure. The rationale is that an identification made closer to the time of the offence when memory is fresher is often more reliable than an in-court identification made months or years later. The identifying officer may testify about the lineup and what the witness did, and the witness may confirm the prior identification. The evidence is admissible for the truth of the identification.
why_A_wrong: Prior identification is a recognized exception to the rule against prior consistent statements. It is not excluded on the basis that it is a prior consistent statement; it is admissible as substantive evidence of identification.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Prior identification is a recognized exception to the rule against prior consistent statements; the officer may testify about the identification procedure and its outcome.
why_C_wrong: There is no 24-hour rule limiting prior identification evidence. The time between the offence and the identification goes to the weight (reliability) of the identification, not to its admissibility. Identifications conducted at any reasonable time after the offence are admissible.
why_D_wrong: In-court identification is not the only admissible form of identification evidence. Prior identification is admissible and is often treated as more reliable than in-court identification made long after the offence. Both forms of identification evidence may be before the trier of fact.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Prior Identification — Exception to Prior Consistent Statement Rule
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Tat (1997), 117 CCC (3d) 481 (Ont. CA); R. v. Baltovich, 2004 CanLII 34404 (Ont. CA)

tested_concepts: prior identification exception to prior consistent statement rule
officer may testify about identification procedure
reliability of identification closer in time

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The Crown seeks to admit a hearsay statement made by the complainant to a nurse shortly after an alleged assault. The complainant is available to testify at trial but the Crown argues that calling her would be traumatic and therefore impractical. Defence objects that necessity has not been established because the complainant is alive and able to testify.
call_of_question: Under the principled approach to hearsay, which of the following correctly describes the necessity criterion?

options.A: Necessity requires only that the declarant is deceased; a live, available witness can never satisfy necessity.
options.B: Necessity is satisfied when it would be reasonably impractical or unduly difficult to obtain the evidence through the declarant's in-court testimony, including situations involving witness unavailability, trauma, or where direct evidence is not practically obtainable.
options.C: Necessity requires that the Crown show the statement is the best evidence available and that no other evidence could prove the same point.
options.D: Necessity is automatically satisfied whenever a witness refuses to cooperate with the Crown.

explanation: Under the principled approach established in Khan and elaborated in subsequent cases, necessity is not limited to situations where the declarant is deceased. The Supreme Court has interpreted necessity broadly: it encompasses situations where the declarant is unavailable (dead, seriously ill, abroad), but also situations where it would be reasonably impractical to obtain the evidence through direct testimony. This includes cases where calling the witness would subject them to significant trauma (particularly in cases involving vulnerable witnesses such as young children), or where the evidence cannot reasonably be obtained in any other way. The necessity criterion reflects the principled approach's flexibility and is not rigidly defined as death or absence alone.
why_A_wrong: Necessity is not limited to situations where the declarant has died. Khan itself involved a child witness who was alive but whose evidence was received through the principled approach based on the practical difficulties of obtaining reliable in-court testimony. Necessity is a flexible concept.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Necessity under the principled approach encompasses unavailability of the witness, practical impracticability of calling them, and situations where the nature of the evidence makes direct testimony unreasonably difficult or traumatic.
why_C_wrong: Necessity does not require the Crown to show the statement is 'the best evidence available.' The principled approach does not apply the best evidence rule. The necessity criterion focuses on the practical availability of the declarant as a witness, not on comparing the hearsay statement to other potential evidence.
why_D_wrong: A witness who refuses to cooperate may satisfy necessity in some circumstances, but necessity is not automatically satisfied by refusal. The court will consider the reasons for and nature of the refusal, and whether other remedies (such as compelling the witness) are available.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Hearsay — Necessity Criterion
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Khan, [1990] 2 SCR 531; R. v. Khelawon, 2006 SCC 57

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The victim of a stabbing was transported to hospital. Before dying from his wounds, the victim told the responding officer: 'Dominic stabbed me — I know I'm going to die.' The victim died the next morning. At Dominic's murder trial, the Crown seeks to admit the victim's dying declaration.
call_of_question: Under the dying declaration exception to the hearsay rule, which of the following conditions is required for the victim's statement to be admissible?

options.A: The victim must have been declared legally dead at the time of making the statement.
options.B: The victim must have had a settled, hopeless expectation of imminent death at the time of the statement, and the statement must relate to the circumstances of his death.
options.C: The dying declaration is admissible only if the victim was under oath at the time the statement was made.
options.D: The dying declaration is admissible only if the accused had the opportunity to cross-examine the victim before death.

explanation: The dying declaration is a traditional hearsay exception that predates the principled approach. For a statement to qualify as a dying declaration at common law, two conditions must be satisfied: (1) the declarant must have been under a settled, hopeless expectation of imminent death at the time of making the statement — the declarant need not use the words 'I am dying' but must subjectively believe death is certain and close; and (2) the statement must concern the circumstances of the declarant's death (the cause of their injury or the identity of the perpetrator). The declarant must in fact die. An oath is not required — the common law substituted the solemnity of impending death for the oath. In criminal proceedings in Canada, dying declarations are also subject to the principled approach as an alternative basis for admission.
why_A_wrong: The declarant need not be legally or medically declared dead at the time of the statement. The dying declaration exception requires a settled belief in imminent death, not actual legal death. The declarant must subsequently die for the exception to apply, but legal pronouncement at the time is not a requirement.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. A dying declaration requires a settled, hopeless expectation of imminent death and a statement about the circumstances of that death. Both conditions must be met.
why_C_wrong: An oath is not required for the dying declaration exception. The traditional rationale is that the gravity of impending death provides a sufficient substitute for the oath — a person facing death would not lie about the identity of their killer.
why_D_wrong: Cross-examination of the dying declarant is not required for the dying declaration exception. The absence of cross-examination opportunity is the very reason the evidence is hearsay — the exception exists to permit admission despite this limitation where the dying declaration conditions are met.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Dying Declarations — Traditional Hearsay Exception
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Booth (1984), 15 CCC (3d) 237 (Ont. CA); Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 709

tested_concepts: no oath required for dying declaration

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: During a domestic dispute, a neighbour heard screaming and immediately called 911. The neighbour testified that moments after the screaming stopped, she saw the complainant run out of her apartment with blood on her face, and the complainant immediately exclaimed: 'He just beat me — he's still inside!' The complainant is unwilling to testify at trial.
call_of_question: The Crown seeks to admit the complainant's spontaneous utterance. Under the res gestae exception, which condition is most critical for admissibility?

options.A: The spontaneous utterance must have been made under oath.
options.B: The contemporaneity of the statement with the startling event — the utterance must be so closely connected to the event that there was no opportunity for fabrication or deliberate reflection.
options.C: The spontaneous utterance must be corroborated by independent physical evidence before it can be admitted.
options.D: The declarant must be permanently unavailable to testify before a spontaneous utterance can be admitted.

explanation: The res gestae exception (also known as the spontaneous utterance exception) admits out-of-court statements that are made contemporaneously with a startling or shocking event, where the declarant is under the stress of the event and has no opportunity to reflect or fabricate. The key rationale is that the psychological stress of the event suppresses the capacity for calculated falsehood, providing a substitute guarantee of reliability. The most critical condition is the contemporaneity/spontaneity requirement: the statement must be part of the event itself or made so closely in time that the declarant was still under the stress of the startling event, with no opportunity for deliberate fabrication. On these facts, the complainant's exclamation immediately upon exiting the apartment — while visibly injured and distressed — is contemporaneous with the event and would satisfy the res gestae exception.
why_A_wrong: Res gestae statements are not required to be made under oath. The exception's guarantee of reliability comes from the spontaneity and stress of the event, not from an oath. This is one of the traditional categorical exceptions that operates without the oath requirement.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Contemporaneity — the close temporal connection between the startling event and the utterance, leaving no opportunity for reflection or fabrication — is the most critical condition for the res gestae exception.
why_C_wrong: Corroboration by physical evidence is not a precondition for the res gestae exception. The reliability of a spontaneous utterance is provided by the circumstances (spontaneity, stress), not by independent corroboration. Requiring corroboration would impose a condition not part of the traditional exception.
why_D_wrong: Permanent unavailability of the declarant is not required for the res gestae exception. Under the traditional categorical approach, the res gestae exception can apply even if the declarant is available but refuses to testify — the admission is based on the nature of the statement, not the declarant's availability. Unavailability may be relevant to necessity under the principled approach but is not a condition of the res gestae exception itself.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Res Gestae — Spontaneous Utterances
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Bedingfield (1879), 14 Cox CC 341; R. v. Ratten, [1972] AC 378 (PC); R. v. Clark (2004), 182 CCC (3d) 1 (Ont. CA)

tested_concepts: no oath required for res gestae

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The Crown seeks to admit a hearsay statement from a young child complainant who is now unavailable to testify. At the voir dire, the trial judge hears evidence that the child was exposed to leading questions by a social worker before making the statement, and that the child's account changed significantly between first disclosure and the recorded statement. The Crown argues the statement is nonetheless sufficiently reliable to be admitted.
call_of_question: At the voir dire stage of a hearsay admissibility ruling, should the trial judge consider the child's inconsistent prior accounts as evidence bearing on threshold reliability?

options.A: No — inconsistencies go to weight, not admissibility; the trial judge must not consider them at the voir dire stage.
options.B: Yes — evidence undermining the circumstances of the making of the statement (including leading questions and changing accounts) is relevant to threshold reliability and may cause the judge to find that admission requirements are not met.
options.C: No — threshold reliability is assessed only by reference to the formal conditions of the statement (under oath, video-recorded), not its contents.
options.D: Yes — but only if the inconsistencies are proved beyond a reasonable doubt by the defence.

explanation: Under R. v. Khelawon, 2006 SCC 57 and R. v. Khan, threshold reliability is assessed by examining the circumstances surrounding the making of the statement. Circumstances that call into question the reliability of the statement — such as leading questions by an interviewer, suggestibility of the witness, significant changes in the account between disclosures, and the conditions of the interview — are highly relevant to threshold reliability. A trial judge may conclude that the circumstances are so contaminated (leading questions, inconsistency) that the statement lacks adequate circumstantial guarantees of reliability and must be excluded. This is different from the judge independently weighing ultimate reliability (which is for the trier of fact); the judge is assessing whether the circumstances surrounding the making of the statement provide adequate substitutes for the traditional safeguards.
why_A_wrong: Inconsistencies and circumstances surrounding the statement are not merely weight issues at the voir dire stage. They bear directly on threshold reliability — whether the circumstances provide adequate guarantees of reliability. A judge may exclude a hearsay statement where the surrounding circumstances are so problematic that threshold reliability is not established.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Circumstances bearing on threshold reliability — including leading questions and changing accounts — are properly considered by the trial judge at the voir dire. They may result in exclusion if threshold reliability is not satisfied.
why_C_wrong: Threshold reliability is not assessed solely by reference to formal conditions (oath, video). Khelawon established that circumstantial reliability — the circumstances surrounding the making of the statement — is the primary basis for threshold reliability assessment. The formal conditions (oath, video-record) relate to procedural reliability, which is a separate pathway.
why_D_wrong: The defence need not prove inconsistencies beyond a reasonable doubt to raise them at the threshold reliability voir dire. The voir dire is a civil-standard proceeding on admissibility. Evidence of circumstances affecting reliability is considered on the whole, not beyond a reasonable doubt.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Hearsay — Threshold Reliability and Contaminating Circumstances
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Khelawon, 2006 SCC 57; R. v. Khan, [1990] 2 SCR 531; R. v. B.(K.G.), [1993] 1 SCR 740

tested_concepts: judge does not assess ultimate reliability

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: s. 276 — What Evidence is Never Admissible
fact_pattern: The accused is charged with sexual assault. He seeks to introduce evidence of the complainant's general sexual history with multiple partners to argue that she is 'the type of person who consents to sex readily' and is therefore less credible when she claims she did not consent.
call_of_question: Under s. 276(1) of the Criminal Code, is this evidence admissible?

options.A: Yes — the complainant's sexual history is relevant to the central issue of consent and is therefore admissible.
options.B: No — s. 276(1) absolutely prohibits the admission of prior sexual activity evidence where it is tendered to suggest the complainant consented to the sexual activity at issue or is less credible.
options.C: Yes — the general sexual history evidence is admissible if it is shown to be relevant on a balance of probabilities.
options.D: No — but the accused may apply under s. 276(2) to have this evidence admitted after a voir dire.

explanation: Section 276(1) of the Criminal Code creates an absolute prohibition — it is not a rule subject to exceptions or a balancing test. Section 276(1) provides that evidence that the complainant engaged in sexual activity other than that at issue is not admissible to support an inference that, by reason of the sexual nature of that activity, the complainant: (a) is more likely to have consented to the sexual activity at issue; or (b) is less worthy of belief. These are the 'twin myths' — the myths that sexually active women are more likely to have consented and are less credible. This absolute prohibition cannot be overcome by a s. 276(2) application. The s. 276(2) process applies to evidence of prior sexual activity tendered for other purposes (not the twin myths); evidence tendered to support the twin myths is categorically inadmissible under s. 276(1) regardless of any application.
why_A_wrong: The relevance of prior sexual activity to consent does not automatically make it admissible. Section 276(1) expressly prohibits the very inference the accused seeks to draw — that prior sexual activity makes the complainant more likely to have consented. This is the definition of the twin myth prohibited by s. 276(1).
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 276(1) creates an absolute prohibition on evidence of prior sexual activity tendered to support either twin myth (more likely to have consented; less worthy of belief). This prohibition is not subject to exceptions or s. 276(2) override.
why_C_wrong: Section 276(1) does not apply a balance of probabilities relevance threshold. It is an absolute prohibition on specific uses of prior sexual activity evidence. The balance of probabilities standard does not apply to the s. 276(1) prohibition.
why_D_wrong: A s. 276(2) application can only succeed for prior sexual activity evidence offered for a purpose other than supporting the twin myths. Evidence tendered to show the complainant 'consents readily' or is 'less credible' because of her sexual history is precisely what s. 276(1) prohibits. No application under s. 276(2) can revive absolutely prohibited evidence.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: s. 276(1) — Absolute Prohibition on Twin Myths Evidence
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 276(1); R. v. Seaboyer, [1991] 2 SCR 577; R. v. Darrach, 2000 SCC 46

tested_concepts: s. 276(1) absolute prohibition — not subject to s. 276(2)
prior sexual activity to show consent or credibility — categorically prohibited

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The accused is charged with sexual assault. He seeks production of the complainant's therapy records from a private therapist, arguing they may contain statements by the complainant that contradict her account of the assault. The complainant objects to disclosure of her therapy records.
call_of_question: Under s. 278.1 of the Criminal Code, which of the following correctly describes the process for an accused to obtain records of a complainant held by a third party?

options.A: The accused is entitled to the records as of right upon demonstrating that the records may be relevant to a trial issue.
options.B: The accused must apply to the trial judge with written notice; the judge conducts a two-stage analysis: first, whether the records should be produced to the judge, and then whether the records (or parts thereof) should be produced to the defence.
options.C: The accused must obtain the consent of the complainant before the records can be produced.
options.D: Third-party records of complainants are categorically inadmissible in sexual assault proceedings.

explanation: Sections 278.1 to 278.91 of the Criminal Code govern the production of records of complainants and witnesses in sexual offence proceedings. Section 278.3 requires the accused to bring a written application with notice to the Crown, the complainant, and the record holder. Section 278.5 then provides a two-stage process: Stage 1 — the judge reviews the application and, if satisfied that the record is likely relevant to an issue at trial or the competence of a witness to testify, orders the record produced to the judge for inspection. Stage 2 — the judge examines the record in camera and, after considering the accused's rights to a full answer and defence (s. 7 Charter) balanced against the complainant's privacy rights (ss. 7 and 8 Charter), may order production of some or all of the record to the accused. Therapy records are expressly recognized in s. 278.1 as records to which this regime applies.
why_A_wrong: The accused is not entitled to third-party records as of right upon showing potential relevance. The s. 278 regime imposes a specific application process and a two-stage judicial balancing exercise before any records reach the accused. Mere potential relevance does not trigger automatic production.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The s. 278 process is a two-stage judicial process: first, production to the judge; second, production to the defence after balancing privacy against full answer and defence.
why_C_wrong: The complainant's consent is not required for production under the s. 278 regime. The complainant has standing to oppose the application and may appear at the hearing, but the decision rests with the judge after the statutory balancing exercise — not with the complainant's consent.
why_D_wrong: Third-party records are not categorically inadmissible in sexual assault proceedings. The s. 278 regime is specifically designed to provide a structured process for producing such records where the accused's rights to a full answer and defence justify production. The regime balances privacy and defence rights, but does not categorically exclude all third-party records.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Third-Party Records — s. 278.1 to 278.91 Criminal Code

exam_trigger_words: privacy vs full answer and defence
tested_concepts: production to judge first then to accused
privacy vs full answer and defence balancing

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Third-Party Records — s. 278 vs s. 276 Distinction
fact_pattern: An accused charged with sexual assault seeks to obtain two categories of evidence: (1) the complainant's prior sexual activity with a third party occurring the night before the assault, and (2) the complainant's records from a psychiatric hospital where she was treated after the assault.
call_of_question: Which statutory regime governs each category of evidence?

options.A: Both categories are governed by s. 276 of the Criminal Code.
options.B: The prior sexual activity is governed by s. 276; the psychiatric records are governed by the common law and require only a demonstration of relevance.
options.C: The prior sexual activity is governed by s. 276 (rape shield); the psychiatric records are governed by s. 278.1 (third-party records regime).
options.D: Both categories are governed by s. 278.1 as records that may undermine the complainant's credibility.

explanation: These two categories of evidence are governed by distinct statutory regimes. Section 276 of the Criminal Code is the 'rape shield' provision that governs evidence of a complainant's prior sexual activity — including sexual activity with other persons. Evidence that the complainant had sexual activity with a third party the night before the assault falls squarely within s. 276. The accused must bring a s. 276.1 application and the judge conducts a voir dire under s. 276.2. By contrast, the complainant's psychiatric hospital records are third-party records held by a person or organization other than the parties. These are governed by s. 278.1 through 278.91 of the Criminal Code, which establishes the two-stage production process. The two regimes are distinct: s. 276 addresses prior sexual activity evidence; ss. 278.1+ address records held by third parties.
why_A_wrong: Section 276 governs only prior sexual activity evidence, not medical or psychiatric records. Applying s. 276 to psychiatric records would be incorrect — those records are governed by the separate s. 278.1 third-party records regime.
why_B_wrong: Psychiatric records of a complainant in a sexual offence proceeding are not governed by the common law relevance standard. They fall squarely within the s. 278.1 definition of 'record' and are subject to the statutory two-stage production process, not a mere relevance showing.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Prior sexual activity — s. 276 (rape shield). Psychiatric records held by a third party — s. 278.1 (third-party records regime). These are two distinct statutory regimes addressing different evidence.
why_D_wrong: Section 278.1 applies to records, not to prior sexual activity evidence. Evidence of prior sexual activity is not a 'record' within the s. 278.1 definition — it is evidence about conduct. The s. 278.1 regime governs documents and records; s. 276 governs evidence of prior sexual conduct.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: s. 276 vs s. 278.1 — Distinct Regimes
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Criminal Code, R.S.C. 1985, c. C-46, s. 276, s. 278.1

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Expert Evidence — Opinion on Ultimate Issue
fact_pattern: The Crown calls a forensic accountant to give opinion evidence that based on his analysis, the accused 'defrauded the company' through a series of disguised transactions. Defence objects that the expert is offering an opinion on the ultimate issue — the very question the jury must decide — and should be precluded from testifying on this point.
call_of_question: Under Canadian law, may an expert witness offer an opinion on the ultimate issue to be decided by the trier of fact?

options.A: No — the ultimate issue rule categorically prohibits experts from expressing opinions on the issues the trier of fact must decide.
options.B: Yes — in Canadian criminal law, an expert may offer an opinion on the ultimate issue, subject to the Mohan criteria; the jury is not bound by the expert's opinion.
options.C: Yes — but only if the accused consents to the expert testifying on the ultimate issue.
options.D: No — in criminal cases, expert evidence on the ultimate issue must be excluded to preserve the jury's function.

explanation: The traditional common law 'ultimate issue rule' prohibited experts from opining on the very question the trier of fact must decide (e.g., 'the accused is guilty' or 'the accused defrauded the company'). However, in R. v. Mohan, [1994] 2 SCR 9, the Supreme Court did not include this prohibition as one of the four admissibility criteria. Canadian courts have generally moved away from a categorical prohibition on ultimate issue opinions. An expert may offer opinions that touch on the ultimate issue in criminal proceedings, provided the Mohan criteria are satisfied (relevance, necessity, no exclusionary rule, qualified expert). The jury retains the function of weighing and accepting or rejecting the expert's opinion — the expert's view on the ultimate issue is evidence for the jury, not binding instruction. The trial judge may use limiting instructions where appropriate.
why_A_wrong: The ultimate issue rule is not a categorical prohibition in Canadian criminal law. Mohan's four criteria do not include an ultimate issue prohibition. Canadian courts permit expert opinions that touch on the ultimate issue where the Mohan criteria are met.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Canadian law does not categorically prohibit expert opinions on the ultimate issue. Such opinions are permissible where the Mohan criteria are satisfied; the jury retains final decision-making authority.
why_C_wrong: Accused consent is not a condition for admitting expert evidence on the ultimate issue. The admissibility of expert evidence is governed by the Mohan criteria and the trial judge's gatekeeper function, not by the accused's consent.
why_D_wrong: Canadian criminal law does not categorically exclude expert evidence on the ultimate issue to preserve the jury's function. The jury's function is preserved by the fact that the expert's opinion is not binding and is subject to the jury's independent assessment. Limiting instructions can address any risk of undue influence.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Expert Evidence — Ultimate Issue Rule
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Mohan, [1994] 2 SCR 9; R. v. J.-L.J., 2000 SCC 51

tested_concepts: ultimate issue rule not categorical prohibition in Canada
Mohan criteria apply to ultimate issue opinions
expert opinion on ultimate issue permissible subject to Mohan

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The defence seeks to call a controversial expert in 'recovered memory therapy' who will testify that the complainant's memories of childhood abuse — recovered during therapy 20 years after the alleged events — are reliable and not the product of suggestion. The Crown argues the scientific basis of recovered memory therapy is contested and that the evidence lacks sufficient scientific reliability.
call_of_question: Under R. v. J.-L.J., 2000 SCC 51 and the post-Mohan gatekeeping framework, what additional consideration does the trial judge apply to determine whether this expert evidence should be admitted?

options.A: The trial judge must accept the expert's testimony once she is qualified as an expert; the jury will assess the weight to give it.
options.B: The trial judge must conduct a threshold reliability analysis, assessing whether the underlying science or methodology is sufficiently reliable, with particular scrutiny for novel scientific evidence.
options.C: The trial judge must determine whether the majority of experts in the field agree with the proposed methodology before admitting the evidence.
options.D: Once the four Mohan criteria are met, the trial judge has no further discretion to exclude the expert evidence.

explanation: In R. v. J.-L.J., 2000 SCC 51, the Supreme Court elaborated on the Mohan framework, emphasizing that the trial judge has a gatekeeping function that includes assessing the threshold reliability of the expert's methodology, particularly for novel or contested science. This threshold reliability analysis — influenced by the Daubert framework in U.S. law — requires the trial judge to consider: whether the theory or technique has been tested; whether it has been subjected to peer review and publication; the known or potential rate of error; and whether it has been generally accepted within the relevant scientific community. For contested sciences like recovered memory therapy, the trial judge must apply particular scrutiny to the scientific foundation before admitting the evidence. Qualifying an expert does not end the inquiry — the underlying methodology must meet a threshold of scientific reliability. This is the judge's gatekeeping role under the expanded Mohan framework.
why_A_wrong: The trial judge is not required to admit all evidence from a qualified expert. The trial judge has an ongoing gatekeeping function that includes threshold reliability review of the methodology. Qualification as an expert is a necessary but not sufficient condition for admission.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under J.-L.J. and the post-Mohan gatekeeping framework, the trial judge must assess threshold reliability of the underlying methodology — particularly for novel or contested science — as part of the admissibility determination.
why_C_wrong: General acceptance by a majority of experts (the Frye standard from U.S. law) is not the Canadian test. Under J.-L.J., general acceptance is one relevant factor among several in assessing threshold reliability, but it is not the sole or determinative criterion. The Canadian framework is more flexible than the Frye standard.
why_D_wrong: Satisfying the four Mohan criteria does not extinguish the trial judge's discretion. The gatekeeping role includes threshold reliability review of the underlying science, which operates in addition to the four Mohan criteria. The Mohan criteria and threshold reliability analysis are complementary layers of the admissibility inquiry.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Expert Evidence — Threshold Reliability and Gatekeeping
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: R. v. Mohan, [1994] 2 SCR 9; R. v. J.-L.J., 2000 SCC 51; White Burgess Langille Inman v. Abbott and Haliburton Co., 2015 SCC 23

tested_concepts: J.-L.J. threshold reliability for expert evidence

subject: criminal_law
subtopic_heading: Prior Inconsistent Statements — K.G.B. Requirements Applied
fact_pattern: At a murder trial, the Crown's key witness recants her earlier statement identifying the accused as the shooter. Her earlier statement was made under oath at a grand jury proceeding (in a foreign jurisdiction) and was audio-recorded but not video-recorded. She had the opportunity to explain the inconsistency on cross-examination at trial.
call_of_question: Under R. v. K.G.B., can this prior statement be admitted as substantive evidence despite being audio-recorded rather than video-recorded?

options.A: No — K.G.B. strictly requires video recording; audio recording is insufficient regardless of circumstances.
options.B: Yes — the K.G.B. criteria are satisfied: oath/solemn affirmation, reliable record (audio meets this where video is unavailable), and opportunity to cross-examine.
options.C: No — statements made in foreign proceedings can never be admitted under K.G.B. as they were not made in Canadian proceedings.
options.D: Yes — once the witness recants, any prior statement she made automatically becomes admissible for its truth.

explanation: In R. v. K.G.B., the SCC required that the prior statement be made under oath and that there be a reliable record of it, with the paradigm case being a video recording that allows the trier of fact to observe demeanour. However, the court recognized that the video-recording requirement reflects the need for a 'reliable memorialization' of the statement. Post-K.G.B. case law has accepted that audio recordings can constitute a reliable record where video is not available, provided the audio recording adequately captures the content of the statement. On these facts, the statement was made under oath at a formal proceeding (equivalent to solemnity), was audio-recorded (reliable record), and the witness had the opportunity to explain the inconsistency at trial. Courts applying the principled approach have admitted audio-recorded statements where the K.G.B. spirit is otherwise satisfied. The audio recording may satisfy the 'reliable record' requirement in the circumstances.
why_A_wrong: K.G.B. did not hold that video recording is an absolute requirement — it described video recording as the paradigm case for reliable memorialization. Subsequent cases have accepted audio recordings and other forms of reliable records where video was not available and the spirit of the K.G.B. criteria is otherwise satisfied.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. An audio recording can satisfy the 'reliable record' requirement of K.G.B. where video was unavailable and the statement meets the other criteria (oath; opportunity to cross-examine). The principled approach is flexible.
why_C_wrong: K.G.B. does not limit admissibility to statements made in Canadian proceedings. The principled approach focuses on the reliability indicators of the statement itself (oath, record, opportunity to cross-examine), not the jurisdiction in which the statement was made. A foreign proceeding conducted under oath with a reliable record can satisfy K.G.B.
why_D_wrong: Recantation alone does not make any prior statement automatically admissible for its truth. The K.G.B. criteria must still be satisfied. Recantation provides the trigger (inconsistency) for the cross-examination process and the application to admit the prior statement, but the admissibility requirements must still be met.

source_reference.subject: Criminal Law
source_reference.heading: Prior Inconsistent Statements — K.G.B. Audio Recording

tested_concepts: K.G.B. reliable record requirement — not limited to video
audio recording can satisfy reliable record
oath + record + cross-examine opportunity = K.G.B. met

subject: criminal_law
fact_pattern: The accused is charged with sexually assaulting three different complainants on separate occasions over a two-year period. All three incidents involved the accused meeting vulnerable women at a bus station, offering them accommodation, and then sexually assaulting them in the same manner. The Crown seeks to introduce evidence of all three incidents as similar fact evidence to prove the identity of the perpetrator and a common system.
call_of_question: Under R. v. Handy, 2002 SCC 56, which of the following factors most strongly supports admissibility of the evidence of all three incidents as similar fact evidence?

options.A: The fact that the accused was convicted of two of the three incidents previously.
options.B: The degree of similarity between the incidents — the same location, same modus operandi, same victim profile — which provides strong circumstantial evidence beyond mere propensity.
options.C: The fact that the Crown has three complainants rather than one increases reliability.
options.D: The long time span between offences reduces prejudice and therefore increases admissibility.
